sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle, presents with a history of injuring her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She reports catching her ski and falling, resulting in her knee popping and experiencing pain ever since.
She was unable to bear weight and had to be taken down by the ski patrol.
She describes the pain as feeling like the knee will buckle, particularly on the outside, and is unable to bear weight or fully straighten the knee.
She denies numbness or tingling in her toes.
She is an active person who chases her two 4-year-old twins and has a supportive husband, but he works a job that requires him to be away for periods of time.
Past medical history: The patient is otherwise generally healthy, with no history of high blood pressure, diabetes, or other significant medical conditions.
Review of systems: The patient denies any additional symptoms in other organs or systems.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking ibuprofen for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
She has decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, and palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse.
There is no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that the patient has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee.
Problem list: ACL rupture; knee pain; knee effusion; knee edema.
Plan:
I will order a knee MRI to confirm the suspected ACL rupture.
I will continue the patient on ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain relief.
I will also recommend a knee brace to provide additional support.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment to discuss the MRI results and further management.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in a few days to review the MRI results and discuss further treatment options.
